The statute clearly relieves the teacher of any duty during this period of time and prohibits the
district and its administration from requiring the teacher to engage in any other activity the
administration determines to be useful and important. Dr. Grigorski, last week you stated that your
position has always been to follow the Texas Education Code, which is great. But you suggested that
if teachers had complaints, we should take them up with our chain of command. Principals, area
superintendents, or you directly, which I trust would be effective if it were affecting just a few
teachers in campuses.

[18:30–19:00]
However, since the start of this 24-25 school year, 171 teachers from 56 KDISD campuses have
reported regularly losing at least one planning period a week. This is 78% of KDISD elementary
schools, 61% of KD junior highs, and 80% of KD high schools for a total of 74% of campuses across
the district that have teachers regularly losing state protected planning time.

[19:00–19:30]
And this is just from a short survey. Imagine what the actual number is. These numbers are alarming
and clearly not a coincidence or a one off, nor is it a problem isolated to individuals in any way.
This issue originates at the district level. The mandate for these planning periods was given
without considering the legal implications, and now the responsibility is unfairly being swept down
to campus administrators to fix a compliance issue created by district administration. We've raised
this concern multiple times with district administrators, including directly to area
superintendents, yet the response has been silence, dismissal, or even

[19:30–20:00]

to violate the Texas Education Code, I urge you to stand behind your previous statement. Follow the
code, follow the law, make it abundantly clear to every teacher on every campus that mandated group
planning time is now optional and on our terms because it cannot be mandated.

Since the TEA takeover, Westbriar has faced relentless instability. Teachers are leaving, leadership
is inconsistent, and morale is at an all time low. A thriving school is built on stability,
community, and trust. Yet those have been systematically dismantled at Westbriar. Parents, students,
and teachers have voiced concerns repeatedly, but we are ignored. On March 7th, we sent a letter
outlining these issues, signed by nearly over 400 parents, students, and invested community members.
We kept student and staff names anonymous to protect them from retaliation. HISD leadership can no
longer dismiss the

[43:00–43:30]
Dismiss this as just a loud few voices. The time for action is now. Thank you. Our next speaker is
uh Anna Heinzelman, number 11. And if numbers 12 and 13 wouldn't mind coming forward. Yes, ma'am,
please go ahead. Hi, my name is Dr. Anna Heinzelman. I'm a professor and I'm a parent at West Briar
Middle School. We are alarmed by the ineffective and damaging treatment of staff, which has created
a punitive and bullying culture after the forced resignation of our principal last year.

[43:30–44:00]
turnover reached 47%. This year we expect even more losses, including veteran teachers and
administrators who have been critical to Westbrier's success. These educators bring years of
experience, strong community relationships, and deep institutional knowledge. Yet they are being
dismissed. Forced into rigid lesson structures, and given little flexibility to meet their students'
diverse needs. Staffing levels have dropped to just 79% of what they were last year. Our teachers
deserve respect, they deserve support.

here in KDISD. As you know, under state law, teachers are entitled to a minimum of 45 minutes of
planning time during each school day. This is not just a record.

[20:30–21:00]
This is not just a recommendation, it is a legal right. However, at least 78% of elementary campuses
on our district are currently facing a situation where this legally required planning time is being
reduced or taken away entirely. It is outlined clearly in the staff handbooks. Teachers are losing
two planning periods each week. To make matters worse, arts are also being scheduled during planning
times on the other days, further depriving teachers of the time they need to prepare lessons for
their students' success. This theft of planning time has gone on for far too long, and I stand here
today to say enough is enough. By scheduling additional obligations during teachers' legally
required

stretched thin. At this moment, the district owes its teachers over five days of compensatory time
just for the twice weekly planning periods that have been stolen from teachers this school year.
This is a considerable amount of time, and it was time that was legally guaranteed but
systematically stolen from teachers. In addition, I want to point out that next year's master.
